figures.gloss: The primordial creator who emerged from Lake Titicaca to fashion the cosmos, breathing life into stone giants before making humanity. He walked the Andes as a wandering beggar, establishing moral order before disappearing across the Pacific Ocean.
figures.facet: He is a classic 'deus otiosus' who delegated the active maintenance of the universe to Inti and others. His wandering as an impoverished old man tests human hospitality before he permanently departs.
figures.provenance: Juan de Betanzos, 'Narrative of the Incas' (1557); Pedro Sarmiento de Gamboa.

figures.gloss: The sovereign Sun God and direct divine ancestor of the Sapa Inca. Housed in the golden Qorikancha, he enforced the imperial state religion and received the highest offerings, including vast herds of llamas, to keep the empire ascendant.
figures.facet: Inti is inseparable from imperial, political legitimacy. He is not a distant cosmic fire but a literal, genealogical patriarch whose daily transit ensures the agricultural and political survival of Tawantinsuyu.
figures.provenance: Garcilaso de la Vega, 'Comentarios Reales de los Incas' (1609).

figures.gloss: The Moon Goddess, sister and consort to Inti, and patroness of women, the feminine cycles, and the ritual calendar. Eclipses were terrifying events where a cosmic creature attacked her, requiring humans to shout and throw weapons to protect her.
figures.facet: She represents the silver, temporal counterbalance to Inti's golden eternity. Her waxing and waning directly calculate the ritual months, intertwining celestial mechanics with women's physical bodies.
figures.provenance: Bernabé Cobo, 'History of the Inca Empire' (1653).

figures.gloss: The immanent, ever-present Earth Mother who sustains all agricultural and human life. She operates on strict principles of cosmic reciprocity, requiring constant ritual feedings of chicha, coca leaves, and fat to remain benevolent.
figures.facet: She is not a passive, serene biosphere, but a hungry, active participant who can cause earthquakes or swallow people if her reciprocal offerings are neglected. Her fertility comes at a literal, metabolic price.
figures.provenance: Pablo Joseph de Arriaga, 'The Extirpation of Idolatry in Peru' (1621); modern Andean ethnography.

figures.gloss: The fierce deity of thunder, lightning, and rain, envisioned as a man in shining garments holding a war club and a sling. He draws water from the celestial river of the Milky Way and shatters a cosmic water jug to cast down rain.
figures.facet: Rain in the Andes is a violent, percussive act. Illapa combines the martial ferocity of a warrior with the life-giving necessity of irrigation, commanding fear and desperate worship during severe droughts.

figures.gloss: A regional supreme deity of the central Andes, born from five falcon eggs. A master of storms and snow, he waged a cataclysmic territorial war against the fire deity Huallallo Carhuincho, expanding his cult through heroic, topographical terraforming.
figures.facet: He represents the localized, deeply rooted 'huaca' system that predates and resisted total assimilation by the Inca. He embodies the living, aggressive spirit of a specific mountain range, demanding exclusive local devotion.

figures.gloss: The Mother of Waters, encompassing the ocean, coastal fishing grounds, and the subterranean springs. She is the ultimate reservoir in the Andean hydraulic cycle, receiving all rivers and redistributing moisture back to the sky.
figures.facet: In a steep, arid geography, she is the guarantor of flow. She represents the bottom half of the cosmic water cycle, maintaining the vital circulation of 'camaquen' (life force) through the universe's veins.
figures.provenance: Bernabé Cobo, 'History of the Inca Empire' (1653).

figures.gloss: A messianic myth-hero born of the traumatic Spanish conquest. Though the colonizers beheaded him and buried his head in Cusco, his body is secretly growing back underground; when complete, he will resurrect the Inca realm.
figures.facet: A profound synthesis of pre-Columbian regenerative cycles and post-conquest resistance. The myth transforms the literal execution of indigenous leaders into an apocalyptic, underground gestation of sovereignty.
figures.provenance: 20th-century Quechua oral traditions (collected by José María Arguedas, 1956).

figures.gloss: The sovereign of the Uku Pacha, the inner earth and underworld, commanding minerals, caves, and deep subterranean forces. He is the master of the dangerous, potent energies that lie beneath the soil.
figures.facet: Originally, he was an amoral, powerful entity of the deep earth who could be bargained with, particularly by miners. The post-conquest demonization of Supay represents the violent overlay of Christian absolute evil onto Andean spatial dualism.
figures.provenance: Ludovico Bertonio, 'Vocabulario de la lengua Aymara' (1612); modern Diablada dances.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Solar fathers who dictate the strict calendrical and agricultural order of the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Distant creator deities who establish the initial cosmic architecture but remain aloof from daily human affairs.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The direct, attested predecessor to the Inca creator deity, establishing the exact iconographic posture and celestial authority millennia earlier.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The continued Andean reverence for the sea's bounty, manifesting later as the Inca mother of the ocean.
Intra-tradition: The primary celestial dyad of sun and moon, governing male and female domains and intertwining the imperial calendar.
Intra-tradition: The dual aspects of the earth—its fertile, life-giving upper crust (Pachamama) and its deep, dangerous, mineral-rich bowels (Supay).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Solar deities who serve as both the literal sun and the ultimate source of dynastic, imperial legitimacy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Supreme solar deities from whom the imperial family claims direct, literal physical descent.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The high god who establishes the architecture of the cosmos before retreating, leaving daily administration to lesser gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Distant, supreme celestial patriarchs whose ancient worship was somewhat displaced by more immediate, systemic natural forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Earth mothers intimately tied to soil fertility who demand strict moral and ritual observance to prevent disaster.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primordial Earth personified as a female entity, foundational to all life but capable of terrifying destructive wrath.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Mountain-dwelling storm and rain gods of the Americas whose life-giving waters require intense, sometimes bloody, propitiation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Thunder deities who strike the earth with lightning, heavily associated with martial prowess and high mountains.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Storm and mountain deities who secure cosmic dominance by physically battling and defeating rival elemental gods.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Dismembered kings whose hidden, buried bodies hold the regenerative promise of future cosmic restoration.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The prophesied savior figure who will emerge at the end of the current age to execute a total world-reversal.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The eschatological concept of a broken world being perfectly restored and cleansed of its corrupted history.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Sovereigns of the underworld and masters of mineral wealth, originally morally neutral but widely feared by the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lords of the pre-Columbian American underworlds, governing the deep earth and the realm of the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Female sovereigns of the cold ocean deeps who control the bounty of the sea and must be respected by fishers.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Lunar deities who govern the night and feminine cycles, operating in direct counterpart to a primary solar god.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A lunar goddess closely tied to the measurement of time, nocturnal beauty, and the feminine sphere.
GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme maternal sovereigns of the ocean, revered for their vast fertility and their ultimate power over the watery deep.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are thunder and storm spirits invoked not just for bringing rain, but specifically to wash away sickness and spiritually purify the community.
GOLD SEAM: The deified essence of the ocean's power, petitioned directly by coastal communities for safe passage and bountiful catches.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision the Earth as a living, conscious mother requiring active reciprocity and ecological reverence from humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The wandering civilizer who sculpts the landscape, molds humanity, and establishes moral order before departing.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The living Earth conceptualized as an entity capable of violently throwing off its abusers to heal itself.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A messianic figure of the colonized whose return is prophesied to overturn the colonial order and restore indigenous sovereignty.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme, life-sustaining mother figures deeply tied to the land who became heavily syncretized with the Virgin Mary.

Both ground a living, volcanically active homeland in a goddess of the earth itself — Pele in Kīlauea's pit, Pachamama in the Andean ground that shakes — but Pachamama is the nurturing tilled earth where Pele is fire and fury; a loose earth-goddess rhyme, flagged.

The feminine seated in the moon — Hina who climbed the moonbeam to beat her kapa there, Mama Killa the Inca silver Moon-mother; the lunar goddess as the bright female counter to the sun, imagined in unrelated worlds.

GOLD SEAM: Earth personified as the great mother who must be asked leave before the land is worked and who receives the dead — Igbo Ala against Andean Pachamama, the world's body reverenced across unrelated families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The personification of the sea as a maternal but dangerous power requiring constant appeasement.

Neighbouring Andean and Araucanian suns: both the daytime sky-power of the east — but Inti is the dynastic solar ancestor of an empire, while Antü is one married pole of a conjugal sky, the contrast of imperial sun against household sun.
Two South-American moon-wives of the sun, mistresses of the night and the calendar — Küyen wedded to Antü as Mama Killa to Inti; the lunar consort of a sky-couple reached on both sides of the Andes.
GOLD SEAM: The living, vertical earth personified as an absolute, demanding female sovereign who requires no sky-father to validate her rule.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are invoked directly as the literal, immediate biosphere and living soil, grounding ritual in ecological reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The wandering Andean civilizer who shapes the landscape and establishes the moral order of society.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme Andean sun god who acts as the celestial anchor for earthly theocratic authority.
GOLD SEAM: Both view the Earth as a living, reciprocating entity that must be energetically balanced with humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are personifications of the earth as an ancient, sustaining mother-figure who directly provides the agricultural and botanical necessities for human survival.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the vast, overwhelming, and deeply maternal matrix of the ocean and deep waters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize the planet as a total, self-sustaining living system that integrates all biological and geological processes.

Sun-fathers at the head of a maize-and-mountain people, met with dawn offering and reckoned the source of life — the Hopi Tawa and the Andean Inti as two New-World solar fathers of agriculturalists, independently central.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme feminine essence of the ocean, revered as both a nurturing mother of aquatic life and a volatile sovereign of the deep.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The profound equation of physical earth and soil with conscious, demanding spiritual custodianship.
GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme solar deities utilized as ideological engines to enforce imperial expansion, unity, and state survival across diverse conquered peoples.

Mothers of the waters from whom life's increase comes — Atabey of the sweet springs, moon and birth and Mama Qucha the mother-sea — the mother-of-waters joint reached on the Antillean and Andean sides of the Americas.

Both are South-American mother-of-waters figures, but Mama Cocha is the great benevolent Mother Sea implored for calm and fish, while Iara is the freshwater enchantress who takes men under; flagged as a divergent water-mother parallel.

Two South-American makers who shape a first humankind and then withdraw — Viracocha walking off across the western sea, Ñamandú unfolding the world by hymn and receding into the winds; the creator who recedes, on either side of the continent.
GOLD SEAM: Andean traditions view the Earth and mountains as inherently alive and divine; Aetherius views the mountains merely as inert batteries requiring external, alien energy to become holy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The sacred, maternal embodiment of the South American earth, requiring propitiation before any disruption of the soil.

dossier.essence: The imperial religion of the Inca state (Tawantinsuyu, c. 1400–1533) laid over a far older Andean substrate of huaca worship shared by Quechua- and Aymara-speaking peoples. Its core: a sacred landscape of person-like powers — Sun, Thunder, Earth, mountains, springs, ancestors — bound to humans by strict reciprocity, with the Sapa Inca ruling as the Sun's own descendant.
dossier.cosmos: The world divides into hanan pacha (the upper world), kay pacha (this world), and uku pacha (the inner world), crossed by flows of water and of camaquen, the animating force that makes each thing what it is. Creation is credited to Viracocha, who emerged at Lake Titicaca, but the working cosmos runs on feeding: rain, fertility, and order are repaid with chicha, coca, textiles, and blood. The honored dead were not sent away but kept — as mummies (mallki) they remained present, fed, and consulted, the royal mummies still holding court in Cusco.
dossier.practice: A professional priesthood served the state cult of Inti at the Qorikancha in Cusco, with chosen women (aqllakuna) brewing and weaving for the gods; from that center radiated the ceque lines organizing hundreds of shrines. Regular rites offered llamas, chicha, and cloth; at moments of crisis the state performed capacocha, the sacrifice of children on high peaks. Festivals such as Inti Raymi fixed the ritual year, while local communities maintained — and in folk-Catholic form still maintain — their own huacas and Pachamama offerings.
dossier.canon: No writing: khipu knotted cords carried record-keeping; the myths survive through early colonial texts — above all the Quechua-language Huarochirí Manuscript (c. 1608) and the Spanish chronicles (Betanzos, Sarmiento de Gamboa, Cobo).
dossier.reflects: Reciprocity as cosmic law — religion as the feeding of a living landscape and a living dead, fused seamlessly with state power.
sources: Frank Salomon & George L. Urioste (trans.), 'The Huarochirí Manuscript' (1991)
Brian S. Bauer, 'The Sacred Landscape of the Inca: The Cusco Ceque System' (1998)
